content.text:
<analysis>
a. **Event type and context**: This is a pull request (PR #204) that was opened with the trigger phrase @claude. The PR adds a salvaged AI-assisted review for P. putida PhaZ gene, including GO annotations, publications, and review files.

b. **Request type**: This is a code review request. The user asked me to review the pull request and provide feedback on code quality, best practices, potential bugs, performance considerations, security concerns, and test coverage.

c. **Key information**: 
- PR adds 7 new files for P. putida PhaZ gene review
- Files include publications (PMID records), UniProt data, GOA annotations, notes, and an AI review YAML
- Copilot already provided detailed review comments identifying issues with author names, full text inclusion, and missing PMIDs in evidence
- Total additions: 813 lines, 0 deletions

d. **Main tasks and challenges**:
- Review all 7 added files for quality, adherence to repo standards, and best practices
- Assess the AI review YAML structure and evidence quality
- Check for security issues, particularly around data licensing and full-text inclusion
- Evaluate completeness and accuracy of the review content
- Address the issues already identified by Copilot reviewer

e. **Plan of action**:
- Read and analyze each of the 7 changed files
- Review the repository's CLAUDE.md guidelines for gene review standards
- Provide comprehensive feedback on each file
- Focus on the structural issues, data quality, and adherence to project standards
- Update GitHub comment with detailed review findings

f. **Limitations**: I can perform a thorough code/content review but cannot run tests or linting commands without proper setup. I'll focus on manual review of content quality and adherence to documented standards.
</analysis>

input.body: ### PR Review: PSEPK PhaZ Gene Review Reviewing this salvaged P. putida PhaZ gene review for code quality, best practices, and adherence to project standards. #### Review Tasks - [ ] Read repository guidelines and standards - [ ] Review publication files for quality and licensing issues - [ ] Analyze gene review structure and content - [ ] Check AI review YAML for evidence quality - [ ] Assess data completeness and accuracy - [ ] Provide comprehensive feedback [View job run](https://github.com/ai4curation/ai-gene-review/actions/runs/23419557508)

content.content:
1→# AI instructions for ai-gene-review
     2→
     3→## Project Overview
     4→
     5→This project is for managing AI reviews of gene annotations. The overall goal is to both
     6→review existing GO annotations using strict defined criteria, and to summarize the resulting
     7→high quality annotations.
     8→
     9→## Repository layout
    10→
    11→src/
    12→  ai_gene_review/
    13→    schema/
    14→      gene_review.yaml <-- linkml schema for review docs
    15→genes/
    16→  human/
    17→    CFAP300/
    18→      CFAP300-notes.md <-- keep your notes here
    19→      CFAP300-ai-review.yaml <-- the output of your AI review
    20→      CFAP300-notes.md <-- your own notes, edit this, use it like a journal and keep appending.
    21→      CFAP300-uniprot.txt <-- the uniprot record downloaded from the UniProt website (do not edit this file)
    22→      CFAP300-goa.tsv <-- the GO annotation downloaded from the QuickGO API (do not edit this file, no need to view it)
    23→      CFAP300-deep-research.md <-- the deep research for the gene review (do not edit this file, it is automatically generated for you)
    24→      CFAP300-citations.md <-- the citations for the gene review (do not edit this file)
    25→      CFAP300-bioinformatics/ <-- the folder for any bioinformatics analyses (create this if necessary)
    26→      CFAP300-predictions-review.yaml <-- review of computational predictions NOT in GOA (optional)
    27→    <other genes>/
    28→      ...
    29→  worm/
    30→    lrx-1/
    31→  SCHPO/  <-- other orgs use uniprot species codes
    32→      ...
    33→publications/
    34→  PMID_123456.md <-- cached publication text (this should be available for you, DO NOT EDIT)
    35→reactome/
    36→  R-HSA-NNNNNN.md <- cached reactome entry
    37→
    38→You can regenerate the derived files by running commands like:
    39→
    40→```
    41→just fetch-gene human CFAP300 
    42→```
    43→
    44→This makes the uniprot, goa, and publications files. It will also create a stub for the ai-review.yaml if it doesn't exist.
    45→
    46→After making edits to the ai-review.yaml file, you can validate the file by running:
    47→
    48→```
    49→just validate human CFAP300
    50→```
    51→
    52→This ensures that the yaml file is consistent with the schema in `src/ai_gene_review/schema/gene_review.yaml`, and also
    53→that it implements best practices.
    54→
    55→To validate all files, run:
    56→
    57→```
    58→just validate-all
    59→```
    60→
    61→files will be written to reports/validation-all.tsv 
    62→
    63→* All gene symbols follow the standard nomenclature for that species. For human, should be HGNC symbols.
    64→
    65→## Gene Review Guidelines
    66→
    67→Your job is to review existing annotations for a gene, following GO guidelines, and synthesize these with what is known in the literature,
    68→and what can be inferred bioinformatically. The general structure of the review is as follows:
    69→
    70→```yaml
    71→id: <uniprot id>
    72→gene_symbol: <HGNC symbol>
    73→taxon:
    74→  id: <NCBI taxon id>
    75→  label: <taxon label>
    76→description: <your own summary of the gene>
    77→references:
    78→  <all relevant reference, including both literature and bioinformatics sources; includes all but not limited to those used in existing_annotations>
    79→existing_annotations:
    80→  <review of existing annotations, using the GO guidelines; should include all annotations from goa.csv>
    81→core_functions:
    82→  <your own summary of the core functions of the gene>
    83→proposed_new_terms:
    84→  <suggestions for new GO terms>
    85→suggested_questions:
    86→  <suggested questions for experts>
    87→suggested_experiments:
    88→  <suggested experiments to elucidate gene function>
    89→```
    90→
    91→See the schema and existing files for more details.
    92→
    93→When creating a new review, always make sure files are in place:
    94→
    95→`just fetch-gene <organism> <gene>`
    96→
    97→This creates the uniprot, goa files, and a stub for the gene review.
    98→
    99→The next thing is for you to do deep research on that gene and make a GENE-notes.md file, with citations inline (e.g [PMID:12345 title, "<supporting text>"]). In some cases this will have been done ahead of time for you.
   100→
   101→## Reviewing existing annotations
   102→
   103→You should start with the ai-review.yaml file, where stubs are created for you (from `just fetch-gene <organism> <gene>`). Any PMID cited should be in the publications folder, e.g. `publications/PMID_123456.md`. Review these.
   104→
   105→A deep research file should already be present in the gene folder, e.g. `genes/SPECIES/GENE/GENE-deep-research-PROVIDER.md` (where PROVIDER is openai, falcon, perplexity, etc.). This should contain a summary of the literature, and any other information you found useful for the review. There may be irrelevant information in the citations, that is OK. If this is not present, you can generate one using:
   106→
   107→```bash
   108→just deep-research SPECIES GENE --provider perplexity
   109→```
   110→
   111→This will create a file like `GENE-deep-research-perplexity.md`. You can also make your own notes in `genes/SPECIES/GENE/GENE-notes.md`.
   112→
   113→Be aware that many GO terms are over-annotations. You should also not take existing annotations as gospel, whether experimental or bioinformatic.
   114→always use a holistic synthesized picture of the gene function that you have obtained from your research.
   115→
   116→```yaml
   117→ActionEnum:
   118→    permissible_values:
   119→      ACCEPT:
   120→        description: Accept the existing annotation as-is, no modifications, and retain as representing the core function of the gene
   121→      KEEP_AS_NON_CORE:
   122→        description: Keep the existing annotation as-is, but mark it as non-core. For pleiotropic genes, this may be the developmental
   123→          processes, or other processes that are not the core function of the gene.
   124→      REMOVE:
   125→        description: Remove the existing annotation, as it is unlikely to be correct based on combined evidence
   126→      MODIFY:
   127→        description: The essence of the annotation is sound, but there are better terms to use (use in combination with proposed_replacement_terms).
   128→          if the term is too general, then MODIFY should be used, with a proposed replacement term for the correct specific function.
   129→          sometimes terms can also be overly specific and contorted, so in some cases you might want to generalize
   130→      MARK_AS_OVER_ANNOTATED:
   131→        description: The term is not entirely wrong, but likely represents an over-annotation of the gene
   132→      UNDECIDED:
   133→        description: The annotation is not clear, and the reviewer is not sure what to do with it. ALWAYS USE THIS IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO ACCESS
   134→          RELEVANT PUBLICATIONS
   135→      NEW:
   136→        
   137→```      
   138→
   139→## Tools
   140→
   141→Use the OLS MCP to find relevant ontology terms, if the terms you need are not in existing_annotations.
   142→
   143→## Curation guidelines
   144→
   145→Avoid the term `protein binding`, this doesn't tell us anything about the actual function. Instead find a more
   146→informative MF term (e.g for adapter function)
   147→
   148→## Isoform and Negation Tracking
   149→
   150→The system tracks isoform-specific GO annotations and NOT (negated) annotations:
   151→
   152→```yaml
   153→existing_annotations:
   154→# Isoform-specific annotation
   155→- term:
   156→    id: GO:0045892
   157→    label: negative regulation of DNA-templated transcription
   158→  evidence_type: IDA
   159→  original_reference_id: PMID:9815658
   160→  isoform: P19544-1  # UniProt isoform ID - only present for isoform-specific annotations
   161→  review:
   162→    summary: ...
   163→
   164→# NOT annotation (negated)
   165→- term:
   166→    id: GO:0045893
   167→    label: positive regulation of DNA-templated transcription
   168→  evidence_type: IDA
   169→  original_reference_id: PMID:9815658
   170→  negated: true  # This is a NOT annotation
   171→  isoform: P19544-1
   172→  review:
   173→    summary: WT1 does NOT activate transcription...
   174→```
   175→
   176→- **`isoform`**: UniProt isoform ID (e.g., `P19544-1`) - indicates what isoform was tested
   177→- **`negated`**: Set to `true` for NOT annotations (e.g., `NOT|involved_in` qualifier in GOA)
   178→
   179→**Important**: Just because an annotation was made on a specific isoform doesn't mean the function is isoform-specific.
   180→The `isoform` field indicates what was tested, not necessarily what is unique to that isoform.
   181→
   182→To backfill isoform info on existing reviews:
   183→```bash
   184→just backfill-isoforms human WT1
   185→just backfill-isoforms-organism human  # all genes in organism
   186→```
   187→
   188→See `docs/isoform_tracking.md` for full documentation and `projects/ISOFORMS.md` for genes with notable isoform-specific functions.
   189→
   190→## Bioinformatics analyses
   191→
   192→In some cases, it may be useful to do additional bioinformatics analyses. To validate gene function. Here are some guidelines:
   193→
   194→For a gene-specific analysis, the folder is `<genes/specues/GENE>-bioinformatics/`. You should make this be reproducible. You can do a `uv init`
   195→here and `uv add` for any packages you need.
   196→
   197→Never hardcode the results of the script, it's OK for a script to return inconclusive results. Never ever make up results.
   198→
   199→**IMPORTANT**: DO NOT write scripts that just tell users to go to a website. If you cannot programmatically call a tool (e.g., requires API keys, browser interaction, or authentication you don't have), then:
   200→1. Be honest about this limitation upfront
   201→2. Write scripts that can parse/process the output from the tool if the user runs it manually
   202→3. Never write misleading code that pretends to do something it doesn't actually do
   203→
   204→You can summarize the results of the bioinformatics analyses in the `<GENE>-bioinformatics/RESULTS.md` file. This can be referenced as any other reference
   205→as e.g `file:<SPECIES>/<GENE>/bioinformatics/RESULTS.md`.
   206→
   207→## Reviewing Computational Predictions
   208→
   209→The `GENE-predictions-review.yaml` file reviews computational/ML predictions that are NOT
   210→in the curated GOA/UniProt annotations. This is separate from `existing_annotations` in the
   211→main review file.
   212→
   213→Use the `PredictionReview` class (validated with `-C PredictionReview`):
   214→
   215→```bash
   216→uv run linkml-validate -s src/ai_gene_review/schema/gene_review.yaml -C PredictionReview genes/ECOLI/yciO/yciO-predictions-review.yaml
   217→```
   218→
   219→### Prediction sources
   220→
   221→Track predictions from: DeepECTF, PANTHER/IBA, InterPro2GO, CLEAN, GloEC, MAPred, or any
   222→other computational method that produces GO or EC predictions.
   223→
   224→### Assessment categories (from de Crécy-Lagard et al. 2025, PMID:40703034)
   225→
   226→| Code | CS | Description |
   227→|------|-----|-------------|
   228→| COR  | 2   | Correct novel prediction |
   229→| CNN  | 2   | Correct but Not Novel (already in training data) |
   230→| LSP  | 2   | Less Precise than existing annotation |
   231→| UNC  | 1   | Uncertain - cannot validate or refute |
   232→| PLI  | 0   | Paralog Incorrect - wrong paralog subfamily |
   233→| NPI  | 0   | Nonparalog Incorrect - refuted by evidence |
   234→| REP  | 0   | Repetition - frequency-biased prediction |
   235→
   236→### Error types
   237→
   238→- `PARALOG_OVERANNOTATION` - Wrong paralog subfamily (Type 6)
   239→- `PATHWAY_CONTEXT_IGNORED` - Predicted pathway absent from organism
   240→- `FREQUENCY_BIAS` - Model defaults to high-frequency training labels
   241→- `IN_VITRO_NOT_IN_VIVO` - In vitro activity doesn't match biological function
   242→- `TRAINING_DATA_CONTAMINATION` - Prediction already in training data
   243→- See schema for full list
   244→
   245→## General guidelines
   246→
   247→* NEVER guess identifiers for terms, genes, publications. Always use the relevant tools or MCPS, or look them up in derived files.
   248→* For files `<GENE>-notes.md`, use literature deep search, and always record provenance for assertions, e.g `[PMID:12345 "<supporting text>"]`
   249→
   250→## Support code
   251→
   252→There is also support code in `src/ai_gene_review`, see the CLAUDE.md file in that directory for more details
   253→on best practices for working with the code.
   254→
   255→
   256→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it looks malicious. If it does, you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer high-level questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1→id: Q88D24
     2→gene_symbol: phaZ
     3→product_type: PROTEIN
     4→status: DRAFT
     5→aliases:
     6→- PP_5004
     7→- phaB
     8→taxon:
     9→  id: NCBITaxon:160488
    10→  label: Pseudomonas putida (strain ATCC 47054 / DSM 6125 / CFBP 8728 / NCIMB 11950
    11→    / KT2440)
    12→description: >-
    13→  PhaZ is the intracellular medium-chain-length polyhydroxyalkanoate (mcl-PHA)
    14→  depolymerase of Pseudomonas putida KT2440. It is associated with PHA granules
    15→  and mobilizes stored polyester during carbon-storage turnover, with knockout
    16→  strains accumulating higher and more persistent PHA levels. The KT2440 locus is
    17→  PP_5004 (UniProt Q88D24); UniProt currently exposes the primary gene name as
    18→  phaB, but the protein description, ESTHER family assignment, and Pseudomonas
    19→  literature identify the gene product as PhaZ.
    20→existing_annotations:
    21→- term:
    22→    id: GO:0004806
    23→    label: triacylglycerol lipase activity
    24→  evidence_type: IEA
    25→  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000118
    26→  review:
    27→    summary: >-
    28→      This automated TreeGrafter annotation assigns a generic lipase substrate
    29→      class that does not match the biology of PP_5004. The protein is curated in
    30→      UniProt as a poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) depolymerase, and the relevant GO
    31→      term already exists as GO:0050527.
    32→    action: MODIFY
    33→    reason: >-
    34→      Replace the triacylglycerol-specific activity term with the dedicated PHA
    35→      depolymerase term. PhaZ mobilizes intracellular mcl-PHA granules, not
    36→      glycerolipids.
    37→    proposed_replacement_terms:
    38→    - id: GO:0050527
    39→      label: poly(3-hydroxyoctanoate) depolymerase activity
    40→    supported_by:
    41→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/phaZ/phaZ-uniprot.txt
    42→      supporting_text: SubName: Full=Poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) depolymerase
    43→      reference_section_type: DATABASE_ENTRY
    44→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/phaZ/phaZ-notes.md
    45→      supporting_text: The best current molecular-function term is GO:0050527 poly(3-hydroxyoctanoate) depolymerase activity rather than triacylglycerol lipase activity.
    46→      reference_section_type: LITERATURE_REVIEW
    47→- term:
    48→    id: GO:0046503
    49→    label: glycerolipid catabolic process
    50→  evidence_type: IEA
    51→  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000118
    52→  review:
    53→    summary: >-
    54→      This process term is misleading for phaZ. PhaZ acts in intracellular PHA
    55→      mobilization at PHA granules, whereas glycerolipid catabolism implies a
    56→      different substrate class and cellular context.
    57→    action: MODIFY
    58→    reason: >-
    59→      GO:0042620 poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) metabolic process is the closest
    60→      existing biological-process term. A more specific catabolic child term would
    61→      be preferable, but the current glycerolipid annotation should not be kept.
    62→    proposed_replacement_terms:
    63→    - id: GO:0042620
    64→      label: poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) metabolic process
    65→    supported_by:
    66→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/phaZ/phaZ-notes.md
    67→      supporting_text: GO:0046503 glycerolipid catabolic process is misleading because intracellular PHA mobilization is not glycerolipid catabolism; GO:0042620 poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) metabolic process is closer, but GO currently lacks a direct poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) catabolic process term for intracellular PHA mobilization.
    68→      reference_section_type: LITERATURE_REVIEW
    69→- term:
    70→    id: GO:0050527
    71→    label: poly(3-hydroxyoctanoate) depolymerase activity
    72→  evidence_type: IDA
    73→  review:
    74→    summary: >-
    75→      This is the best available molecular-function term for phaZ. Biochemical
    76→      work on the P. putida ortholog established PhaZ as an intracellular
    77→      granule-associated mcl-PHA depolymerase and identified 3-hydroxyoctanoate
    78→      as the major hydrolysis product.
    79→    action: NEW
    80→    reason: >-
    81→      This term captures the substrate specificity of the enzyme substantially
    82→      better than the current TreeGrafter lipase annotation.
    83→    supported_by:
    84→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/phaZ/phaZ-notes.md
    85→      supporting_text: PhaZ is the intracellular granule-associated depolymerase that mobilizes stored medium-chain-length PHA in KT2440.
    86→      reference_section_type: LITERATURE_REVIEW
    87→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/phaZ/phaZ-notes.md
    88→      supporting_text: Biochemical work on the highly similar KT2442 ortholog showed that 3-hydroxyoctanoate monomer is the main hydrolysis product and classified PhaZ as an endo/exo-mcl-PHA depolymerase.
    89→      reference_section_type: LITERATURE_REVIEW
    90→- term:
    91→    id: GO:0042620
    92→    label: poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) metabolic process
    93→  evidence_type: IMP
    94→  review:
    95→    summary: >-
    96→      Genetic perturbation data show that phaZ is part of in vivo PHA turnover.
    97→      Deleting phaZ increases and stabilizes PHA accumulation, while PhaZ dosage
    98→      influences flux through the PHA cycle.
    99→    action: NEW
   100→    reason: >-
   101→      This is the nearest existing biological-process term for intracellular PHA
   102→      mobilization in KT2440.
   103→    supported_by:
   104→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/phaZ/phaZ-notes.md
   105→      supporting_text: Deletion of phaZ increases and stabilizes PHA accumulation, showing that PP_5004 participates in poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) metabolic process in vivo.
   106→      reference_section_type: LITERATURE_REVIEW
   107→- term:
   108→    id: GO:0070088
   109→    label: polyhydroxyalkanoate granule
   110→  evidence_type: IDA
   111→  review:
   112→    summary: >-
   113→      PhaZ is a granule-associated intracellular depolymerase rather than a
   114→      soluble glycerolipid catabolic enzyme. GO has a dedicated cellular component
   115→      term for the PHA granule, which is the relevant site of action.
   116→    action: NEW
   117→    reason: >-
   118→      Granule association is central to PhaZ function and is more informative than
   119→      generic cytoplasmic localization.
   120→    supported_by:
   121→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/phaZ/phaZ-notes.md
   122→      supporting_text: PhaZ is associated with polyhydroxyalkanoate granules rather than glycerolipid catabolism.
   123→      reference_section_type: LITERATURE_REVIEW
   124→references:
   125→- id: GO_REF:0000118
   126→  title: TreeGrafter-generated GO annotations
   127→  findings: []
   128→- id: file:PSEPK/phaZ/phaZ-uniprot.txt
   129→  title: UniProt entry Q88D24 for the KT2440 PHA depolymerase locus PP_5004
   130→  full_text_unavailable: false
   131→  findings:
   132→  - statement: UniProt identifies Q88D24 as a poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) depolymerase
   133→    supporting_text: SubName: Full=Poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) depolymerase
   134→    reference_section_type: DATABASE_ENTRY
   135→  - statement: UniProt maps the entry to KT2440 locus PP_5004
   136→    supporting_text: GN   OrderedLocusNames=PP_5004
   137→    reference_section_type: DATABASE_ENTRY
   138→  - statement: UniProt still exposes phaB as the primary gene name despite the depolymerase assignment
   139→    supporting_text: GN   Name=phaB
   140→    reference_section_type: DATABASE_ENTRY
   141→- id: file:PSEPK/phaZ/phaZ-notes.md
   142→  title: phaZ literature and ontology review notes
   143→  full_text_unavailable: false
   144→  findings:
   145→  - statement: PhaZ is the intracellular granule-associated depolymerase that mobilizes stored medium-chain-length PHA in KT2440
   146→    supporting_text: PhaZ is the intracellular granule-associated depolymerase that mobilizes stored medium-chain-length PHA in KT2440.
   147→    reference_section_type: LITERATURE_REVIEW
   148→  - statement: The most specific existing molecular-function term is GO:0050527
   149→    supporting_text: The best current molecular-function term is GO:0050527 poly(3-hydroxyoctanoate) depolymerase activity rather than triacylglycerol lipase activity.
   150→    reference_section_type: LITERATURE_REVIEW
   151→  - statement: phaZ deletion increases and stabilizes PHA accumulation in vivo
   152→    supporting_text: Deletion of phaZ increases and stabilizes PHA accumulation, showing that PP_5004 participates in poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) metabolic process in vivo.
   153→    reference_section_type: LITERATURE_REVIEW
   154→  - statement: PhaZ is associated with PHA granules
   155→    supporting_text: PhaZ is associated with polyhydroxyalkanoate granules rather than glycerolipid catabolism.
   156→    reference_section_type: LITERATURE_REVIEW
   157→- id: PMID:12534463
   158→  title: Complete genome sequence and comparative analysis of the metabolically versatile Pseudomonas putida KT2440
   159→  full_text_unavailable: true
   160→  findings: []
   161→core_functions:
   162→- molecular_function:
   163→    id: GO:0050527
   164→    label: poly(3-hydroxyoctanoate) depolymerase activity
   165→  directly_involved_in:
   166→  - id: GO:0042620
   167→    label: poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) metabolic process
   168→  locations:
   169→  - id: GO:0070088
   170→    label: polyhydroxyalkanoate granule
   171→  description: >-
   172→    PhaZ is the intracellular granule-associated depolymerase that hydrolyzes
   173→    stored medium-chain-length polyhydroxyalkanoate in KT2440. Its activity
   174→    supports PHA mobilization rather than synthesis, and loss of phaZ causes
   175→    elevated and sustained PHA accumulation.
   176→  supported_by:
   177→  - reference_id: file:PSEPK/phaZ/phaZ-notes.md
   178→    supporting_text: PhaZ is the intracellular granule-associated depolymerase that mobilizes stored medium-chain-length PHA in KT2440.
   179→    reference_section_type: LITERATURE_REVIEW
   180→  - reference_id: file:PSEPK/phaZ/phaZ-notes.md
   181→    supporting_text: Deletion of phaZ increases and stabilizes PHA accumulation, showing that PP_5004 participates in poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) metabolic process in vivo.
   182→    reference_section_type: LITERATURE_REVIEW
   183→proposed_new_terms:
   184→- proposed_name: poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) catabolic process
   185→  proposed_definition: >-
   186→    The chemical reactions and pathways resulting in the breakdown of
   187→    poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate), an intracellular carbon- and energy-storage
   188→    polyester.
   189→  justification: >-
   190→    GO has the specific molecular-function term GO:0050527 and the broader
   191→    process term GO:0042620, but it lacks a direct catabolic-process term for
   192→    intracellular PHA mobilization by depolymerases such as PhaZ.
   193→  proposed_parent:
   194→    id: GO:0042620
   195→    label: poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) metabolic process
   196→  supported_by:
   197→  - reference_id: file:PSEPK/phaZ/phaZ-notes.md
   198→    supporting_text: GO:0046503 glycerolipid catabolic process is misleading because intracellular PHA mobilization is not glycerolipid catabolism; GO:0042620 poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) metabolic process is closer, but GO currently lacks a direct poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) catabolic process term for intracellular PHA mobilization.
   199→    reference_section_type: LITERATURE_REVIEW
   200→suggested_questions:
   201→- question: Which of the additional putative PhaZ paralogs in the KT2440 genome are active depolymerases, and do they target distinct PHA chemistries or growth states?
   202→- question: What nutritional or regulatory signals control phaZ expression and granule recruitment during transitions from PHA accumulation to mobilization?
   203→suggested_experiments:
   204→- description: Measure PHA amount and monomer composition over time in wild type, clean Delta phaZ, and complemented PP_5004 strains during nitrogen starvation and refeeding on octanoate or lignin-derived substrates.
   205→  experiment_type: genetic perturbation plus polymer quantification
   206→  hypothesis: Loss of phaZ blocks intracellular PHA mobilization and prolongs high polymer loads after accumulation.
   207→- description: Localize epitope-tagged or antibody-detected PhaZ across growth phases by fluorescence microscopy or immunogold electron microscopy and correlate localization with granule morphology and turnover.
   208→  experiment_type: localization assay
   209→  hypothesis: PhaZ is recruited to PHA granules specifically during mobilization phases.
   210→- description: Test purified PP_5004 against mcl-PHA latex, defined hydroxyalkanoate oligomers, triacylglycerols, and p-nitrophenyl esters to refine substrate specificity for GO curation.
   211→  experiment_type: biochemical enzyme assay
   212→  hypothesis: PP_5004 prefers intracellular mcl-PHA substrates and does not behave as a generic triacylglycerol lipase.
   213→

1→# phaZ Gene Review Notes
     2→
     3→## Identifier Mapping
     4→
     5→- In KT2440, the literature gene name `phaZ` maps to locus `PP_5004` and UniProt `Q88D24`, even though the UniProt gene line currently says `Name=phaB`. [file:PSEPK/phaZ/phaZ-uniprot.txt, "SubName: Full=Poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) depolymerase"; "GN   Name=phaB"; "GN   OrderedLocusNames=PP_5004"; "DR   ESTHER; psepu-PHAZ; PHA_depolymerase_arom."]
     6→
     7→## Core Function
     8→
     9→- PhaZ is the intracellular granule-associated depolymerase that mobilizes stored medium-chain-length PHA in KT2440. [JBC 2007 PDF, "first formal biochemical evidence that PhaZ is certainly an intracellular mcl-PHA depolymerase"; https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/ae3e/1d24baa56e7222a02f12b2b3b75ee84cee02.pdf]
    10→- Biochemical work on the highly similar KT2442 ortholog showed that 3-hydroxyoctanoate monomer is the main hydrolysis product and classified PhaZ as an endo/exo-mcl-PHA depolymerase. [JBC 2007 PDF, "the monomer of HO was identified as the main product of PhaZ hydrolysis"; "classify PhaZ as an endo/exo-mcl-PHA depolymerase"; https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/ae3e/1d24baa56e7222a02f12b2b3b75ee84cee02.pdf]
    11→- The best current molecular-function term is GO:0050527 poly(3-hydroxyoctanoate) depolymerase activity rather than triacylglycerol lipase activity. [AmiGO GO:0050527, "poly(3-hydroxyoctanoate) depolymerase activity"; https://amigo.geneontology.org/amigo/term/GO%3A0050527]
    12→
    13→## Physiological Role
    14→
    15→- Deletion of phaZ increases and stabilizes PHA accumulation, showing that PP_5004 participates in poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) metabolic process in vivo. [PMC3918157, "the Delta phaZ knockout phenotype suggests that the PhaZ depolymerase is a major determinant of PHA accumulation and maintenance in the cell"; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3918157/]
    16→- PhaZ is associated with polyhydroxyalkanoate granules rather than glycerolipid catabolism. [JBC 2007 PDF, "Identification of PhaZ as granule-associated protein"; PMC6922519, "the gene encoding the PHA depolymerase (phaZ; PP_5004) was deleted"; https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/ae3e/1d24baa56e7222a02f12b2b3b75ee84cee02.pdf; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6922519/]
    17→
    18→## GO Review Implications
    19→
    20→- GO:0004806 triacylglycerol lipase activity is a substrate-misassigned automated annotation and should be replaced by GO:0050527. [file:PSEPK/phaZ/phaZ-uniprot.txt, "SubName: Full=Poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) depolymerase"; AmiGO GO:0050527, "poly(3-hydroxyoctanoate) depolymerase activity"; https://amigo.geneontology.org/amigo/term/GO%3A0050527]
    21→- GO:0046503 glycerolipid catabolic process is misleading because intracellular PHA mobilization is not glycerolipid catabolism; GO:0042620 poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) metabolic process is closer, but GO currently lacks a direct poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) catabolic process term for intracellular PHA mobilization. [AmiGO term graph, "GO:0042620 poly(3-hydroxyalkanoate) metabolic process"; JBC 2007 PDF; https://amigo.geneontology.org/amigo/term/GO%3A0008152; https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/ae3e/1d24baa56e7222a02f12b2b3b75ee84cee02.pdf]
    22→

     3→title: 'Biochemical evidence that phaZ gene encodes a specific intracellular medium
     4→  chain length polyhydroxyalkanoate depolymerase in Pseudomonas putida KT2442: characterization
     5→  of a paradigmatic enzyme.'
     6→authors:
     7→- de Eugenio LI
     8→- Garci A P
     9→- Luengo JM
    10→- Sanz JSM
    11→- Roma N JS
    12→- Garci A JL
    13→- Prieto MAA

    18→---
    19→
    20→# Biochemical evidence that phaZ gene encodes a specific intracellular medium chain length polyhydroxyalkanoate depolymerase in Pseudomonas putida KT2442: characterization of a paradigmatic enzyme.
    21→**Authors:** de Eugenio LI, Garci A P, Luengo JM, Sanz JSM, Roma N JS, Garci A JL, Prieto MAA
    22→**Journal:** J Biol Chem (2007)
    23→**DOI:** [10.1074/jbc.M608119200](https://doi.org/10.1074/jbc.M608119200)
    24→
    25→## Abstract
    26→
    27→1. J Biol Chem. 2007 Feb 16;282(7):4951-4962. doi: 10.1074/jbc.M608119200. Epub 
    28→2006 Dec 14.
    29→
    30→Biochemical evidence that phaZ gene encodes a specific intracellular medium 
    31→chain length polyhydroxyalkanoate depolymerase in Pseudomonas putida KT2442: 
    32→characterization of a paradigmatic enzyme.
    33→
    34→de Eugenio LI(1), Garci A P(1), Luengo JM(2), Sanz JSM(3), Roma N JS(4), Garci A 
    35→JL(1), Prieto MAA(5).
    36→
    37→Author information:
    38→(1)Departamento de Microbiologi´a Molecular, Centro de Investigaciones 
    39→Biolo´gicas, Consejo Superior de Investigaciones Cienti´ficas (CSIC), C. Ramiro 
    40→de Maeztu, 9, 28040 Madrid.
    41→(2)Departamento de Bioqui´mica y Biologi´a Molecular, Universidad de Leo´n, 
    42→24007 Leo´n.
    43→(3)Instituto de Biologi´a Molecular y Celular, Universidad Miguel Herna´ndez, 
    44→Av. Universidad, s/n. 03202 Elche (Alicante), and the.
    45→(4)Instituto de Ciencia y Tecnologi´a de Poli´meros, CSIC, C. Juan de la Cierva 
    46→3, 28006 Madrid, Spain.
    47→(5)Departamento de Microbiologi´a Molecular, Centro de Investigaciones 
    48→Biolo´gicas, Consejo Superior de Investigaciones Cienti´ficas (CSIC), C. Ramiro 
    49→de Maeztu, 9, 28040 Madrid. Electronic address: auxi@cib.csic.es.
    50→
    51→Polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs) can be catabolized by many microorganisms using 
    52→intra- or extracellular PHA depolymerases. Most of our current knowledge of 
    53→these intracellular enzyme-coding genes comes from the analysis of short chain 
    54→length PHA depolymerases, whereas medium chain length PHA (mcl-PHA) 
    55→intracellular depolymerization systems still remained to be characterized. The 
    56→phaZ gene of some Pseudomonas putida strains has been identified only by 
    57→mutagenesis and complementation techniques as putative intracellular mcl-PHA 
    58→depolymerase. However, none of their corresponding encoded PhaZ enzymes have 
    59→been characterized in depth. In this study the PhaZ depolymerase from P. putida 
    60→KT2442 has been purified and biochemically characterized after its 
    61→overexpression in Escherichia coli. To facilitate these studies we have 
    62→developed a new and very sensitive radioactive method for detecting PHA 
    63→hydrolysis in vitro. We have demonstrated that PhaZ is an intracellular 
    64→depolymerase that is located in PHA granules and that hydrolyzes specifically 
    65→mcl-PHAs containing aliphatic and aromatic monomers. The enzyme behaves as a 
    66→serine hydrolase that is inhibited by phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride. We have 
    67→modeled the three-dimensional structure of PhaZ complexed with a 
    68→3-hydroxyoctanoate dimer. Using this model, we found that the enzyme appears to 
    69→be built up from a corealpha/beta hydrolase-type domain capped with a lid 
    70→structure with an active site containing a catalytic triad buried near the 
    71→connection between domains. All these data constitute the first biochemical 
    72→characterization of PhaZ and allow us to propose this enzyme as the paradigmatic 
    73→representative of intracellular endo/exo-mcl-PHA depolymerases.
    74→
    75→DOI: 10.1074/jbc.M608119200
    76→PMID: 17170116 [Indexed for MEDLINE]
    77→
